perturbation.original_text: Each Party will maintain complete and accurate records of all activities performed by or on behalf of such Party under the Research Program and all Inventions made or generated by or on behalf of such Party in the performance of the Research Program. Such records will be in sufficient detail and in good scientific manner appropriate for patent and regulatory purposes. Each Party will provide the other Party with the right to inspect such records, and upon request will provide copies of all such records, to the extent reasonably required for the exercise or performance of such other Party's rights or obligations under this Agreement, provided that any information disclosed under this Section 2.7 will be subject to the terms and conditions of Section 5. Each Party will retain such records for at least three (3) years following expiration or termination of this Agreement or such longer period as may be required by applicable law or regulation.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will endeavor to maintain records of activities performed under the Research Program and Inventions made. Such records should be in a manner that is generally appropriate. Each Party will allow the other Party to inspect records if feasible. Each Party will retain records for a reasonable period after the Agreement ends.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates 'complete and accurate records' maintained in a 'good scientific manner appropriate for patent and regulatory purposes.' The modified text weakens this to 'endeavor to maintain records' in a 'generally appropriate' manner, and 'if feasible,' making the obligation vague and difficult to enforce. The retention period is also reduced from a specified period to a 'reasonable period,' which is ambiguous.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 58 - Good Laboratory Practice for Nonclinical Laboratory Studies
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR 搂 58.190 mandates the retention of records for specific periods, particularly concerning nonclinical lab studies which could easily encompass elements of the Research Plan. The modification to 'reasonable period' directly contradicts the prescriptive retention periods defined in the CFR, potentially jeopardizing regulatory compliance.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding record-keeping, contradicting the prescriptive nature of 21 CFR 搂 58.190, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The original contract specified 'complete and accurate records' for a set duration, whereas the changed text uses vague terms like 'endeavor to maintain records' for a 'reasonable period,' undermining the enforceability and compliance with regulatory standards. The scraped snippet references 21 CFR 58.190, which the Law Explanation notes mandates specific retention periods which the modified text contradicts.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party hereby represents and warrants to the other Party that: (a) it is duly organized, validly existing, and in good standing under the laws and regulations of the jurisdiction in which it is organized; (b) it has the requisite power and authority and the legal right to enter into this Agreement and to perform its obligations hereunder; (c) it has taken all requisite action on its part to authorize the execution and delivery of this Agreement and the performance of its obligations hereunder; (d) this Agreement has been duly executed and delivered by such Party and constitutes a legal, valid and binding obligation of such Party, enforceable against such Party in accordance with its terms; and (e) the execution, delivery and performance of this Agreement by it do not conflict with any agreement, instrument or understanding, oral or written, to which it is a party, or to which it is bound, and it will not enter into any agreement, instrument or understanding, oral or written, that conflicts with the rights and obligations of this Agreement during the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party represents that it believes it is generally in good standing under the laws and regulations of its jurisdiction and that it has generally taken action to authorize the execution of this Agreement. Each Party believes this Agreement is a valid obligation and that performance of this Agreement should not conflict with other agreements.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear and strong warranty regarding the legal standing and authority of each party. By using phrases like 'believes it is generally in good standing,' 'generally taken action,' and 'should not conflict,' the modified text weakens the warranty. This introduces significant ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of these assurances, as it becomes difficult to prove a breach based on subjective beliefs rather than objective facts.
perturbation.law_explanation: 8 Del. C. 搂 102(a)(1) requires that a corporation's certificate of incorporation include the name of the corporation. The wording must be exact. The original text accurately states that Anixa is a Delaware corporation. By weakening the representation to a 'belief' about being in good standing and having taken requisite action, it creates uncertainty about the corporation's actual compliance with fundamental organizational requirements. This would contradict the premise that the company is appropriately registered and capable of legally entering into the agreement under Delaware law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces definitive representations and warranties with subjective beliefs and expectations. The original contract states each party 'is duly organized, validly existing, and in good standing,' while the changed text states that each party 'believes it is generally in good standing.' This introduces ambiguity regarding the party's actual legal standing. The LLM Law Explanation suggests this change weakens the assurance of compliance with 8 Del. C. 搂 102(a)(1), which requires a corporation's certificate of incorporation to include the corporation's name accurately. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by detailing the required contents of the certificate of incorporation under Delaware law, implying strict adherence to these requirements is necessary. The original text ensures this adherence, while the modified text weakens this assurance, potentially contradicting the need for exactness in corporate registration.

perturbation.original_text: OntoChem will own, and Anixa hereby assigns to OntoChem, all right, title and interest in and to all Inventions directed to (a) any methods of generating or screening compound libraries and (b) the Rejected Hit Compounds (including the composition, use or manufacture thereof), in the case of this clause (b), effective as of the Selection Deadline (collectively (clauses (a) and (b)), "OntoChem Inventions"). As between the Parties, OntoChem will exclusively control the filing, prosecution, maintenance and enforcement of any patents and patent applications claiming OntoChem Inventions.
perturbation.changed_text: OntoChem will generally own the Inventions related to generating or screening compound libraries and the Rejected Hit Compounds. OntoChem will usually control the filing, prosecution, maintenance and enforcement of patent applications.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that OntoChem *will own* all right, title, and interest. Replacing this with 'OntoChem will generally own' creates ambiguity about the extent of ownership. Similarly, stating that OntoChem 'will usually control' patent prosecution introduces uncertainty. Does this mean sometimes they won't, and who decides when? This undermines the clarity of the intellectual property ownership and control provisions.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S.C. 搂 261 states that patents have the attributes of personal property.  Assignments of property must be explicitly clear to be enforceable. By making the ownership of patents and their prosecution "general", it becomes unclear if they have complete control over the ownership of patents arising from it or if the rights are actually assigned. A lack of clarity of what 'generally' or 'usually' means in practice violates the conditions of 35 U.S.C. 搂 261.

Ownership; assignment Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces definitive terms like 'will own' and 'will exclusively control' with ambiguous terms like 'generally own' and 'usually control.' This introduces uncertainty regarding the ownership and control of patents, which directly contradicts the requirement of clarity in property assignments as stated in 35 U.S.C. 搂 261, which indicates that 'patents shall have the attributes of personal property' and assignments must be clear. The modification weakens the explicit nature of the assignment, potentially making it unenforceable.
